61.A: Placental abruption triggers disseminated intravascular coagulation with consumption of fibrinogen; cryoprecipitate is the richest source of fibrinogen (and factors VIII/XIII) to correct the coagulopathy.
61.B: The bleeding here is driven by a consumptive coagulopathy with low fibrinogen, not primarily by thrombocytopenia; platelets are given only if the count is critically low.
61.C: Albumin is a volume expander that contains no clotting factors and does not treat the coagulopathy.
61.D: Iron corrects iron-deficiency anemia over weeks and has no role in acute hemorrhage.
61.hy: Placental abruption can cause DIC with fibrinogen depletion; cryoprecipitate is the best source of fibrinogen for replacement.
61.one: Abruption + DIC + ongoing bleeding = Cryoprecipitate (replace fibrinogen).

62.A: Diseased kidneys fail to produce erythropoietin, causing the normocytic normochromic anemia of chronic kidney disease.
62.B: Myelodysplastic syndrome can occur in the elderly but is not suggested here; the small, chronically diseased kidneys point to CKD.
62.C: Iron deficiency causes a microcytic anemia; the anemia of CKD is characteristically normocytic.
62.D: Anemia of chronic inflammation is a distractor; in CKD the dominant mechanism is reduced renal erythropoietin.
62.hy: Anemia of CKD is normocytic normochromic and driven by reduced renal erythropoietin production; treat with erythropoiesis-stimulating agents plus iron.
62.one: CKD + small kidneys + anemia = Erythropoietin deficiency.

63.A: Oral iron has already failed because active celiac disease impairs duodenal absorption; continuing it will not work.
63.B: Active celiac disease impairs duodenal iron absorption, so oral iron fails; intravenous iron bypasses the gut and corrects the deficiency.
63.C: A liquid oral preparation is still absorbed in the diseased duodenum and cannot overcome malabsorption.
63.D: Increasing the oral dose does not fix the underlying malabsorption.
63.hy: In malabsorption (e.g., celiac disease) or oral-iron failure/intolerance, give intravenous iron to bypass the gut.
63.one: Celiac + failed oral iron = IV iron (bypass malabsorption).

64.A: Vitamin K is not routinely required for an asymptomatic, moderately elevated INR without bleeding; it is reserved for markedly high INR or bleeding.
64.B: For an elevated INR without bleeding, the correct action is to withhold warfarin and recheck the INR in a couple of days.
64.C: Fresh frozen plasma is reserved for serious or life-threatening bleeding, not for asymptomatic elevation.
64.D: Prothrombin complex concentrate is used for major/life-threatening bleeding, not asymptomatic INR elevation.
64.hy: An elevated INR (up to ~10) with no bleeding is managed by withholding warfarin and rechecking; reserve vitamin K/PCC/FFP for higher INR or active bleeding.
64.one: High INR, no bleeding = withhold warfarin and recheck INR.

65.A: High-dose steroids are not the definitive treatment for HELLP; corticosteroids are only an adjunct and do not replace delivery.
65.B: HELLP syndrome (hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets) at term is treated by prompt delivery, the only definitive management.
65.C: Plasma exchange is the treatment for TTP; the microangiopathic hemolysis here is due to preeclampsia/HELLP and resolves with delivery.
65.D: Eculizumab is used for atypical HUS, not HELLP.
65.hy: HELLP syndrome at or near term is treated by delivery; distinguish it from TTP/HUS, which require plasma exchange or eculizumab.
65.one: Term + preeclampsia + hemolysis + low platelets (HELLP) = Deliver.

66.A: An INR below 1.0 provides no anticoagulation and is ineffective.
66.B: An INR of 1.0-1.5 is subtherapeutic and does not protect against thromboembolism.
66.C: The target INR for atrial fibrillation, including valvular AF from rheumatic mitral stenosis, is 2.0-3.0.
66.D: An INR above 3.0 carries excess bleeding risk and is reserved for certain mechanical valves.
66.hy: Target INR for atrial fibrillation (including valvular AF from rheumatic mitral stenosis) is 2.0-3.0.
66.one: AF (incl. rheumatic MS) on warfarin = INR 2.0-3.0.

67.A: Prophylactic exchange transfusion throughout pregnancy is reserved for severe complications (stroke, acute chest syndrome), not routine uncomplicated disease.
67.B: Uncomplicated sickle cell disease in pregnancy is managed with folic acid and close observation; routine prophylactic transfusion is not indicated.
67.C: Simple transfusion is used for symptomatic anemia or crisis, not as routine prophylaxis.
67.D: Hydroxyurea is teratogenic and contraindicated in pregnancy.
67.hy: Hydroxyurea is contraindicated in pregnancy; uncomplicated sickle cell disease is managed with folic acid and observation, reserving transfusion for complications.
67.one: SCD in pregnancy, no complications = folic acid + close observation (no hydroxyurea).

68.A: Hydralazine is not a first-line agent and causes reflex tachycardia.
68.B: Amlodipine is effective but an ACE inhibitor is preferred in diabetics for renal protection.
68.C: Metoprolol is not first line and can mask hypoglycemia in diabetics.
68.D: ACE inhibitors (ramipril) are first line in diabetics for blood-pressure control and renoprotection, reducing albuminuria.
68.hy: In hypertensive diabetics, ACE inhibitors (or ARBs) are first line for their renoprotective, anti-proteinuric effect.

69.A: An echocardiogram is not the step used to establish a diagnosis of hypertension.
69.B: Starting antihypertensives on a single office reading is premature; confirm the diagnosis first.
69.C: Ambulatory blood-pressure monitoring confirms hypertension and excludes white-coat effect before treatment.
69.D: Repeating the office reading in 3 months delays diagnosis; out-of-office monitoring is the preferred confirmatory test.
69.hy: Diagnose hypertension with out-of-office (ambulatory or home) blood-pressure measurement to exclude white-coat hypertension before starting drugs.
69.one: Elevated office BP = confirm with ambulatory BP monitoring.

70.A: Noninvasive ventilation may be needed if respiratory acidosis persists, but the immediate cause of the drowsiness is over-oxygenation, so reduce the oxygen first.
70.B: Nebulized ipratropium is a bronchodilator and will not reverse CO2 narcosis.
70.C: Uncontrolled high-flow oxygen in COPD causes CO2 retention and narcosis; titrate the oxygen down to a target SpO2 of 88-92%.
70.D: Increasing salbutamol does not address the hypercapnia caused by excess oxygen.
70.hy: In COPD, uncontrolled high-flow oxygen causes CO2 retention and narcosis; titrate oxygen to SpO2 88-92%.
70.one: COPD + high-flow O2 + drowsy = reduce inspired oxygen (target SpO2 88-92%).

71.A: Bronchiectasis produces copious purulent sputum and would show a different clinical picture.
71.B: Bronchial asthma is episodic and reversible, not a chronic daily productive cough in a smoker.
71.C: Pulmonary fibrosis causes a dry cough with clubbing, fine crackles, and a restrictive pattern.
71.D: A productive cough for at least 3 months over 2 consecutive years in a smoker defines chronic bronchitis.
71.hy: Chronic bronchitis is defined clinically as a productive cough for at least 3 months per year for 2 consecutive years.
71.one: Smoker + productive cough 3 months x 2 years = Chronic bronchitis.

72.A: Trans-bronchial biopsy has low yield for interstitial lung disease; a surgical biopsy is used if tissue is needed.
72.B: Diffusion capacity on PFT is reduced but does not identify the radiological pattern.
72.C: High-resolution CT showing a UIP pattern (basal reticulation, honeycombing) is the key diagnostic test for interstitial lung disease/IPF.
72.D: Serum ACE is used for sarcoidosis, not idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.
72.hy: HRCT of the chest is the key investigation for interstitial lung disease; a UIP pattern supports idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.
72.one: Clubbing + basal crackles + reticular shadowing = HRCT chest (UIP).

73.A: High-flow oxygen is dangerous in COPD because it causes CO2 retention.
73.B: An albuterol metered-dose inhaler is less effective than nebulized combination therapy in an acute exacerbation.
73.C: Intravenous aminophylline is not first line and has a narrow therapeutic index.
73.D: Combined nebulized bronchodilators (salbutamol plus ipratropium) are first-line therapy in an acute COPD exacerbation.
73.hy: Acute COPD exacerbation: controlled oxygen (88-92%), nebulized salbutamol + ipratropium, steroids, and antibiotics; avoid high-flow oxygen.
73.one: COPD exacerbation = nebulized salbutamol + ipratropium (controlled O2).

74.A: A chest X-ray is not diagnostic of obstructive sleep apnea.
74.B: Polysomnography (sleep study) confirms obstructive sleep apnea, the likely diagnosis in this obese, somnolent patient.
74.C: Echocardiography assesses cor pulmonale but does not diagnose sleep apnea.
74.D: High-resolution CT of the lung is not relevant to this presentation.
74.hy: Polysomnography (sleep study) is the diagnostic test for obstructive sleep apnea.
74.one: Obese + daytime somnolence + nocturia = Sleep study (OSA).

75.A: At 62 she is below the age 65 threshold used in severity scores, so age itself is not the marker here.
75.B: Significant comorbidity such as diabetes independently predicts worse pneumonia outcomes and is weighted in the Pneumonia Severity Index.
75.C: Leukocytosis is an expected inflammatory response; a very low white cell count would be more ominous than a high one.
75.D: A parapneumonic effusion is a complication, but the comorbidity carries independent prognostic weight in this question.
75.hy: Comorbidities such as diabetes worsen pneumonia prognosis and are weighted in severity tools like the Pneumonia Severity Index.
75.one: Comorbid diabetes = adverse prognostic factor in pneumonia.

76.A: A respiratory fluoroquinolone (moxifloxacin) covers typical and atypical pathogens and is appropriate empiric therapy for severe community-acquired pneumonia.
76.B: Azithromycin monotherapy is insufficient for severe pneumonia; it is used in combination.
76.C: Vancomycin covers only MRSA and is not empiric monotherapy for community-acquired pneumonia.
76.D: Ceftazidime is antipseudomonal with poor pneumococcal and atypical coverage, making it a poor empiric CAP choice.
76.hy: Respiratory fluoroquinolones (moxifloxacin, levofloxacin) cover both typical and atypical CAP organisms.
76.one: Severe CAP empiric monotherapy = respiratory fluoroquinolone (moxifloxacin).

77.A: Sarcoidosis causes bilateral hilar adenopathy, not a persistent consolidation with atypical cells.
77.B: Atypical pneumonia would respond to antibiotics; a non-resolving picture is a red flag against it.
77.C: Allergic pneumonitis is exposure-related and does not yield atypical malignant cells.
77.D: A consolidation that fails to resolve despite antibiotics, with atypical cells on lavage, suggests bronchogenic carcinoma.
77.hy: Non-resolving pneumonia or consolidation despite adequate antibiotics warrants workup for an underlying bronchogenic carcinoma.
77.one: Non-resolving consolidation + atypical cells = Bronchogenic cancer.

78.A: Lung fibrosis is restrictive with reduced lung volumes, not the hyperinflation seen here.
78.B: Hyperinflation (raised total lung capacity and residual volume), hyperresonance, and irreversible airflow obstruction in a smoker indicate emphysema.
78.C: Bronchiectasis presents with chronic copious sputum, which is not described.
78.D: Allergic pneumonitis is restrictive and exposure-related.
78.hy: Emphysema shows hyperinflation (raised TLC and RV), reduced diffusion capacity, and fixed, non-reversible airflow obstruction.

79.A: Oral prednisolone is for acute exacerbations, not for maintenance step-up therapy.
79.B: Inadequate control on a short-acting beta-agonist alone should be stepped up by adding a low-dose inhaled corticosteroid, the preferred controller.
79.C: Low-dose aminophylline is not the preferred controller and has more side effects.
79.D: Inhaled ipratropium is not a first-line controller in asthma.
79.hy: Asthma not controlled on a SABA alone should be stepped up by adding a low-dose inhaled corticosteroid.
79.one: Asthma uncontrolled on salbutamol = add inhaled corticosteroid.

80.A: Sinus tachycardia is a response to the underlying event, not the primary diagnosis.
80.B: Cerebral infarction would produce focal neurological deficits rather than this picture.
80.C: Brain hemorrhage is unlikely without head trauma or focal signs.
80.D: Immobilization after a leg injury (a DVT risk) with sudden confusion, tachycardia, and a clear chest X-ray points to pulmonary embolism causing hypoxia.
80.hy: Sudden dyspnea/hypoxia/tachycardia with a clear chest X-ray in an immobilized patient is pulmonary embolism until proven otherwise.
80.one: Immobile + leg injury + tachycardia + confusion + clear CXR = Pulmonary embolism.

416.A: There is no indication to induce labor at 30 weeks with a reassuring fetal status; expectant management is appropriate.
416.B: Tocolysis is not indicated without contractions, and the patient still needs latency antibiotics.
416.C: Magnesium neuroprotection is considered nearer to delivery or if in labor, and latency antibiotics are still required.
416.D: PPROM management is latency antibiotics (erythromycin plus ampicillin) with antenatal corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity.
416.hy: PPROM before 34 weeks: give antenatal corticosteroids plus latency antibiotics (erythromycin/ampicillin); avoid routine tocolysis.
416.one: PPROM at 30 weeks = corticosteroids + latency antibiotics (erythromycin + ampicillin).

417.A: She is G4P3, a factor, but the standout risk here is macrosomia causing uterine overdistension and atony.
417.B: Labor lasted only 5 hours and was not prolonged.
417.C: A 4600 g macrosomic infant overdistends the uterus, predisposing to atony and postpartum hemorrhage.
417.D: No previous history of postpartum hemorrhage is given.
417.hy: Uterine overdistension (macrosomia, multiple gestation, polyhydramnios) causes atony, the leading cause of postpartum hemorrhage.

418.A: Grand parity is a factor, but multiple gestation is the direct cause of overdistension here.
418.B: Labor lasted 5 hours and was not prolonged.
418.C: The twins weigh 2800 g and 2900 g and are not macrosomic.
418.D: Twins overdistend the uterus, causing atony and postpartum hemorrhage.
418.hy: Multiple gestation overdistends the uterus and is a key risk factor for atonic postpartum hemorrhage.
418.one: Twins + PPH = multiple gestation (overdistension to atony).

419.A: She is G4P3, but the operative factor here is the 16-hour labor.
419.B: Prolonged labor exhausts the myometrium (uterine inertia), causing atony and postpartum hemorrhage.
419.C: A 3000 g baby is a normal weight.
419.hy: Prolonged labor causes myometrial fatigue and uterine atony, a risk factor for postpartum hemorrhage.

420.A: Grand multiparity (6 or more births) predisposes to uterine atony and postpartum hemorrhage.
420.B: Labor lasted 7 hours and was not prolonged.
420.C: A 3000 g baby is a normal weight.
420.hy: Grand multiparity (5 or more deliveries) weakens the myometrium and predisposes to atonic postpartum hemorrhage.

421.A: She is G2P1, not a grand multipara.
421.B: Labor was very short, the opposite of prolonged.
421.C: A 3000 g baby is a normal weight.
421.D: Labor under 3 hours (precipitous) predisposes to uterine atony and postpartum hemorrhage.
421.hy: Precipitous labor (under 3 hours) is a risk factor for atonic postpartum hemorrhage.

422.A: O-negative blood is used only if cross-matched blood is unavailable in a life-threatening bleed; it is not the first organizational step.
422.B: Simply sending tests is too passive for an obstetric emergency.
422.C: Surgical management comes later, only if medical measures fail.
422.D: Postpartum hemorrhage is an emergency: the initial step is to call for help and mobilize resources, including cross-matched blood, while resuscitating.
422.hy: PPH is managed by a simultaneous team approach: call for help, resuscitate (ABC, IV access, fluids/blood), and treat the cause (atony first).
422.one: PPH first step = call for help + secure cross-matched blood (resuscitate).

423.A: Propofol is an anesthetic, not a uterotonic.
423.B: Misoprostol is a prostaglandin uterotonic used to treat atonic postpartum hemorrhage alongside oxytocin and ergometrine.
423.C: Dexamethasone is a steroid with no role in postpartum hemorrhage.
423.D: Magnesium sulfate relaxes the uterus and would worsen atony; it is used for eclampsia, not PPH.
423.hy: Uterotonics for atonic PPH: oxytocin (first line), ergometrine, and prostaglandins (misoprostol/carboprost); avoid magnesium sulfate, which relaxes the uterus.

424.A: Digital examination is avoided in suspected PPROM because of the infection risk.
424.B: A sterile speculum examination confirms rupture of membranes by visualizing pooling of amniotic fluid while avoiding infection.
424.C: Reassuring and discharging is inappropriate with suspected PPROM.
424.D: Ultrasound is supportive, but rupture of membranes is confirmed clinically by the speculum examination.
424.hy: Suspected PPROM: confirm with a sterile speculum exam (pooling, with nitrazine/ferning); avoid digital exams to reduce infection.
424.one: Suspected ruptured membranes = sterile speculum exam (avoid digital).

425.A: Labor causes pain without fever; the fever here points to infection.
425.B: PPROM with maternal fever and uterine/abdominal tenderness indicates intra-amniotic infection (chorioamnionitis).
425.C: Placental abruption causes painful bleeding, not fever.
425.D: Cervical incompetence causes painless dilation, not a febrile illness.
425.hy: PPROM + maternal fever + uterine tenderness (with fetal tachycardia or foul discharge) = chorioamnionitis, requiring delivery and antibiotics.

426.A: A high vaginal swab identifies colonizing organisms (e.g., group B streptococcus) to guide antibiotic therapy in prolonged PPROM and preterm labor.
426.B: Antipyretics with review in 2 hours is inappropriately passive; she is in preterm labor at 31 weeks.
426.C: There is no indication for immediate cesarean (no distress or malpresentation given).
426.D: Fetal blood sampling for pH is not indicated at this stage or gestation.
426.hy: In preterm labor with prolonged PPROM, take a high vaginal swab to guide antibiotics (group B streptococcus/infection) as part of management.
426.one: Preterm labor + prolonged PPROM = high vaginal swab for culture.

427.A: Naloxone is used for neonatal respiratory depression after birth, not while the mother is still in labor.
427.B: The cervix is 9 cm and progressing; augmentation is unnecessary and risky with meconium present.
427.C: Simply waiting is less proactive than optimizing maternal position now.
427.D: With a reassuring fetal trace, left lateral positioning optimizes uteroplacental perfusion while labor progresses.
427.hy: With a reassuring CTG in advanced labor, conservative measures (maternal repositioning, hydration, oxygen) suffice; avoid unnecessary oxytocin.
427.one: Advanced labor + reassuring CTG = reposition mother (conservative).

428.A: Maternal blood pH is not the standard assessment; fetal scalp pH is used, not maternal.
428.B: Oxytocin would worsen late decelerations and should be reduced, not started.
428.C: Immediate cesarean is reserved for persistent or ominous traces; first attempt intrauterine resuscitation.
428.D: Late decelerations reflect uteroplacental insufficiency, so intrauterine resuscitation (left lateral position, IV fluids, oxygen, stop oxytocin) is done first.
428.hy: Late decelerations = uteroplacental insufficiency: intrauterine resuscitation (reposition, fluids, oxygen, stop oxytocin), then deliver if persistent.
428.one: Late decelerations = intrauterine resuscitation first (reposition, fluids, O2).

429.A: Hysterectomy is not indicated for asymptomatic prolapse.
429.B: Posterior vaginal repair is reserved for symptomatic prolapse.
429.C: Asymptomatic prolapse needs no intervention beyond reassurance, with pelvic floor exercises if desired.
429.D: Mesh insertion is not used for asymptomatic prolapse and carries complications.
429.hy: Asymptomatic pelvic organ prolapse is managed conservatively (reassurance, pelvic floor exercises); surgery is for symptomatic cases.

430.A: In an elderly, comorbid, poor surgical candidate, a vaginal pessary is the appropriate initial, non-surgical treatment for procidentia.
430.B: Vaginal hysterectomy is high-risk in this frail patient and is not the initial choice.
430.C: Radical hysterectomy is for malignancy and is inappropriate here.
430.D: Laparoscopic hysterectomy with sacrospinous suspension is major surgery, unsuitable as an initial choice for a frail 90-year-old.
430.hy: A vaginal pessary is first-line for symptomatic prolapse in women who decline or are unfit for surgery.
430.one: Procidentia in a frail elderly woman = vaginal pessary (initial).

431.A: Mild, low-grade symptomatic prolapse is managed conservatively first with pelvic floor exercises, reassurance, and follow-up.
431.B: Anterior and posterior repair is reserved for higher-grade or refractory symptoms.
431.C: Vaginal hysterectomy is too aggressive for grade 2 prolapse with mild symptoms.
431.D: Oral analgesics do not treat prolapse-related discomfort.
431.hy: Mild, low-grade symptomatic prolapse is managed conservatively (pelvic floor muscle training, lifestyle) before surgery.
431.one: Grade 2 prolapse + mild symptoms = pelvic floor exercises (conservative).

432.A: Conservative management has already been tried for 6 months without benefit.
432.B: Symptomatic high-grade prolapse that fails conservative therapy warrants counseling for surgical management.
432.C: Reviewing in 6 months delays care despite failed conservative treatment.
432.hy: Symptomatic prolapse that fails conservative measures (or is high-grade) should be offered surgical management.
432.one: Grade 3 prolapse failing conservative care = counsel for surgery.

433.A: Prolapse in pregnancy is managed conservatively with reassurance and usually improves after delivery.
433.B: Urgent delivery and repair is unindicated and would cause harmful preterm delivery.
433.D: Urgent delivery is inappropriate at 29 weeks for asymptomatic prolapse.
433.hy: Prolapse in pregnancy is managed conservatively and typically resolves after delivery.

434.A: Acute urinary symptoms suggest a UTI, so the urine should be tested first before attributing symptoms to prolapse.
434.B: Vaginal hysterectomy is premature until infection is excluded.
434.C: Transvaginal tape is for stress incontinence, not this acute presentation.
434.D: Anterior and posterior repair is not indicated for acute urinary symptoms.
434.hy: New dysuria, frequency, or urgency warrants urinalysis and culture to exclude a urinary tract infection before other workup.
434.one: Acute urinary symptoms = urinalysis (rule out UTI) first.

435.A: Surgery is never the first step for acute urinary symptoms.
435.B: Antimuscarinic therapy is for overactive bladder, only after infection is excluded.
435.C: Acute urinary urgency and incontinence require urinalysis and culture first to exclude a UTI.
435.D: A beta-3 agonist is an overactive-bladder drug, appropriate only after excluding infection.
435.hy: Acute-onset urinary urgency/incontinence: exclude a UTI with urinalysis and culture before treating as overactive bladder.

436.A: Repeating hCG in a week is inadequate because a fall of less than 15% from day 4 to 7 mandates action now.
436.B: Immediate surgery is reserved for rupture or instability or failed therapy; she is asymptomatic.
436.C: The single-dose protocol allows a repeat dose rather than switching to a multiple-dose regimen.
436.D: When hCG falls less than 15% between days 4 and 7, a second dose of methotrexate is given (single-dose protocol).
436.hy: Single-dose methotrexate for ectopic: check hCG on days 4 and 7; a fall under 15% means repeat the dose; a fall of 15% or more means monitor weekly.
436.one: Ectopic MTX, hCG drop <15% (day 4 to 7) = second MTX dose.

437.A: A fall of 15% or more from day 4 to 7 indicates a response, so continue weekly hCG surveillance.
437.B: Surgery is not needed in a responding, asymptomatic patient.
437.C: A second dose is given only if the fall is less than 15%.
437.D: A multiple-dose regimen is not indicated when the patient is responding to the single dose.
437.hy: With methotrexate, an hCG fall of 15% or more between days 4 and 7 is an adequate response: monitor weekly until negative.
437.one: Ectopic MTX, hCG drop >=15% = weekly hCG follow-up.

438.A: Repeating hCG in a week is unsafe with a markedly rising level.
438.B: A markedly rising hCG indicates failed medical therapy or possible rupture, so surgical intervention is required.
438.C: With this steep rise, a second dose is inadequate and surgery is safer.
438.D: A multiple-dose regimen is inappropriate given the steep rise indicating failure.
438.hy: Failed medical management of an ectopic (rising hCG, rupture, or instability) requires surgical management (salpingectomy/salpingostomy).
438.one: Ectopic MTX with rising hCG (failure) = surgery.

439.A: Ceasing follow-up is premature because hCG is not yet negative.
439.B: Once responding, continue weekly hCG until it is undetectable.
439.C: Surgery is not indicated in a responding patient.
439.D: A second dose is not needed while hCG continues to fall.
439.hy: After successful methotrexate, monitor weekly hCG until undetectable before stopping surveillance.
439.one: Ectopic MTX responding = weekly hCG until negative.

440.A: Giving a second dose before reassessing the anatomy and viability is premature.
440.B: Immediate surgery is reserved for rupture or instability; she may be stable.
440.C: A rising hCG after methotrexate warrants a transvaginal ultrasound to reassess the ectopic (size, cardiac activity, rupture) before deciding on repeat methotrexate versus surgery.
440.D: A treatment decision needs ultrasound reassessment first.
440.hy: A rising hCG after methotrexate for an ectopic should prompt transvaginal ultrasound reassessment before escalating therapy.
440.one: Ectopic MTX with rising hCG = reassess with transvaginal ultrasound.

441.A: Weekly readings until three negatives is an alternative/older protocol; ACOG specifies weekly until normal, then monthly.
441.C: ACOG post-molar surveillance is weekly hCG until undetectable, then monthly for 6 months.
441.D: Monthly readings until three negatives is the wrong frequency.
441.hy: Post-molar surveillance (ACOG): weekly hCG until undetectable, then monthly for 6 months; avoid pregnancy during monitoring.
441.one: Molar pregnancy surveillance = weekly hCG until negative, then monthly x6 months.

442.A: A pelvic ultrasound is not needed with a falling hCG.
442.B: Discharge is premature; surveillance continues for months.
442.C: Confirm normalization with continued weekly hCG before switching to monthly surveillance.
442.D: Monthly monitoring begins only after documented normalization.
442.hy: After hCG normalizes post-molar evacuation, confirm with weekly levels, then monitor monthly for 6 months.
442.one: Molar, hCG just normalized = continue weekly hCG to confirm.

443.A: Continuing weekly surveillance is inadequate because the rising hCG plus a mass signals gestational trophoblastic neoplasia.
443.C: Biopsy is contraindicated because a vaginal GTN metastasis is highly vascular and can hemorrhage massively.
443.D: A rising hCG with a vaginal metastasis indicates GTN: stage with a metastatic workup and do not biopsy the vascular lesion.
443.hy: Vaginal metastases of gestational trophoblastic neoplasia are highly vascular: never biopsy; diagnose GTN by rising hCG and stage with imaging.
443.one: Post-mole rising hCG + vaginal mass = GTN: metastatic workup, no biopsy.

444.A: In-clinic biopsy is contraindicated because the vascular GTN lesion may hemorrhage.
444.B: Postpartum GTN/choriocarcinoma with a vascular mass should be staged with a metastatic workup (hCG, imaging), avoiding biopsy.
444.C: Repeating hCG in a week is too passive for a symptomatic vascular mass.
444.D: Immediate suction evacuation risks hemorrhage and is not the staging approach for GTN.
444.hy: Postpartum bleeding + a vascular vaginal/uterine mass + high hCG = choriocarcinoma; stage with a metastatic workup, do not biopsy.
444.one: Postpartum vascular mass + high hCG = GTN: metastatic workup (no biopsy).

445.A: A hormonal method such as the vaginal ring is far more effective than barrier or spermicide methods.
445.B: The female condom has a high typical-use failure rate.
445.C: The male condom is less effective than hormonal methods.
445.D: Spermicide is the least effective method listed.
445.hy: Hormonal methods (ring, pill, patch, implant, IUD) are more effective than barrier methods or spermicides.
445.one: Most effective reversible option listed = hormonal vaginal ring.

446.A: A size of 4 cm (at or above 3.5-4 cm) reduces methotrexate success and is a relative contraindication.
446.B: An unruptured 4 cm ectopic will not reliably resolve with observation.
446.C: Expectant management is only for small ectopics with declining hCG.
446.D: A 4 cm ectopic exceeds the favorable size for methotrexate, so laparoscopic surgical management is appropriate.
446.hy: Methotrexate for ectopic favors size under 3.5-4 cm, hCG under 5000, no cardiac activity, and hemodynamic stability; larger or higher values favor surgery.
446.one: 4 cm ectopic (too large for MTX) = surgical excision.

447.A: Right salpingotomy alone leaves the diseased right tube and the left hydrosalpinx, which lowers IVF success.
447.B: Right salpingectomy alone treats the ectopic but leaves the hydrosalpinx, which reduces future IVF success.
447.C: Removing the ectopic-bearing right tube and the left hydrosalpinx is best, since a hydrosalpinx markedly lowers IVF success and she will conceive via IVF.
447.D: Intraoperative methotrexate is not the definitive management here.
447.hy: A hydrosalpinx reduces IVF success, so bilateral salpingectomy is advised before IVF when a hydrosalpinx coexists with a tubal ectopic.
447.one: Ectopic + contralateral hydrosalpinx (IVF patient) = remove both tubes.

448.A: Laparoscopy is not indicated without localizing an ectopic in a stable patient.
448.B: Methotrexate cannot be given before locating or confirming an ectopic.
448.C: Reassuring and discharging is unsafe because this could be an early ectopic.
448.D: A pregnancy of unknown location in a stable patient is managed with serial hCG and a repeat ultrasound to determine viability and location.
448.hy: Pregnancy of unknown location (positive test, no sac seen) in a stable patient: serial hCG (48-hour trend) and a repeat ultrasound.
448.one: Positive test + no sac on scan (stable) = repeat hCG + ultrasound in 48 h.

449.A: An inevitable miscarriage requires an open os or bleeding.
449.B: A threatened miscarriage requires bleeding with a closed os.
449.C: Pregnancy of unknown location is excluded because an intrauterine sac is seen.
449.D: An intrauterine sac without a heartbeat at about 5 weeks is too early to confirm nonviability, so it is labeled uncertain viability and rescanned.
449.hy: An early intrauterine sac without cardiac activity that is too early to call nonviable is a pregnancy of uncertain viability; repeat the scan before diagnosing miscarriage.
449.one: Early intrauterine sac, no heartbeat, too early = uncertain viability (rescan).

450.A: A septic miscarriage requires signs of infection.
450.B: Fetal demise (a 9-week CRL with no heartbeat) with a closed os and no expulsion is a missed miscarriage.
450.C: An inevitable miscarriage has an open os with bleeding.
450.D: A threatened miscarriage has a viable fetus with a closed os.
450.hy: Missed miscarriage = a nonviable pregnancy (no cardiac activity at the expected CRL) retained with a closed cervical os.
450.one: CRL 9 weeks, no heartbeat, closed os = Missed miscarriage.

451.A: A retained nonviable pregnancy without expulsion is a missed miscarriage.
451.B: Recurrent miscarriage means three or more consecutive losses, which is not described.
451.C: An incomplete miscarriage involves partial expulsion of products.
451.D: A threatened miscarriage has a viable fetus with bleeding and a closed os.
451.hy: Fetal demise retained without expulsion is a missed miscarriage.
451.one: Fetal death retained, no expulsion = Missed miscarriage.

452.A: An open cervical os with protruding membranes, even with a heartbeat, means the miscarriage cannot be prevented (inevitable).
452.B: An incomplete miscarriage means some products have already passed.
452.C: In a threatened miscarriage the os is closed.
452.D: An anembryonic pregnancy is an empty sac with no embryo, whereas here an embryo with a heartbeat is present.
452.hy: Inevitable miscarriage = an open cervical os (with protruding membranes) with the pregnancy still in utero; it cannot be salvaged.
452.one: Open os + protruding membranes (even with heartbeat) = Inevitable miscarriage.

453.A: An open os with a protruding sac plus systemic illness (feeling unwell) indicates infection, a septic miscarriage.
453.B: A threatened miscarriage has a closed os and is excluded.
453.C: An incomplete miscarriage involves partial expulsion of products.
453.D: An anembryonic pregnancy has no embryo, whereas here there is cardiac activity.
453.hy: Miscarriage with signs of systemic infection (fever, malaise, offensive discharge) is a septic miscarriage: give antibiotics and perform urgent evacuation.

454.A: An oxytocin infusion is not used in first-trimester management like this.
454.B: Admission with antibiotics is for septic miscarriage, and no infection is described.
454.C: Reassurance and review in a week is appropriate only for minor threatened miscarriage; significant bleeding needs closer care.
454.D: With significant bleeding, admit and stabilize the patient and be prepared to proceed to evacuation if the miscarriage becomes inevitable.
454.hy: Threatened miscarriage with heavy bleeding: admit and stabilize; be prepared for evacuation if it progresses to inevitable/incomplete miscarriage.
454.one: Bleeding + closed os + heavy loss = admit, stabilize, prepare for possible evacuation.

455.A: A pelvic assessment evaluates the cervix (open or closed) and quantifies bleeding to classify the miscarriage and guide management.
455.B: Admission for observation is premature before assessment.
455.C: Ultrasound for placental localization is irrelevant at 9 weeks.
455.D: Intravenous progesterone is not standard acute management.
455.hy: First-trimester bleeding: perform a pelvic (speculum/exam) assessment to determine cervical os status and classify the miscarriage.

496.A: Endometritis is an infection with fever and discharge, not chronic menorrhagia with a diffusely enlarged uterus.
496.B: Endometriosis causes dysmenorrhea and dyspareunia but the uterus is typically normal-sized, not diffusely enlarged.
496.C: A tender, diffusely (globularly) enlarged uterus with secondary dysmenorrhea and menorrhagia and a normal endometrium is adenomyosis.
496.D: Uterine sarcoma causes a rapidly enlarging uterus, usually postmenopausal, with abnormal pathology.
496.hy: Adenomyosis = a boggy, diffusely enlarged, tender uterus with secondary dysmenorrhea and menorrhagia; definitive treatment is hysterectomy.
496.one: Tender, diffusely enlarged uterus + menorrhagia + normal endometrium = Adenomyosis.

497.C: Abdominal examination is limited for pelvic pathology.
497.D: A bimanual pelvic (digital) examination yields the most diagnostic information about the pelvic organs.
497.hy: A bimanual pelvic (digital) examination provides the most direct clinical assessment of the uterus and adnexa.
497.one: Highest-yield pelvic assessment = bimanual digital examination.

498.A: 10-12 weeks is the nuchal translucency window, not the detailed anomaly scan.
498.B: 14-16 weeks is too early for a full anatomy survey.
498.C: The detailed fetal anomaly (anatomy) scan is performed at 18-22 weeks.
498.D: 24-28 weeks is later than optimal for the anatomy survey.
498.hy: The detailed mid-trimester fetal anomaly scan is done at 18-22 weeks' gestation.

499.A: Oxygen by mask is part of resuscitation, but repositioning is the immediate first maneuver.
499.B: Monitoring blood pressure is passive and does not resuscitate the fetus.
499.C: Cord prolapse causes variable, not late, decelerations.
499.D: Late decelerations call for left lateral repositioning first to relieve aortocaval compression and improve uteroplacental flow, with fluids, oxygen, and stopping oxytocin.
499.hy: Late decelerations = uteroplacental insufficiency: intrauterine resuscitation starting with left lateral repositioning.

500.A: Verapamil is not a first-line antihypertensive in pregnancy.
500.B: Nifedipine is used but methyldopa is the classic first-line oral agent.
500.C: Hydralazine is an IV agent for acute severe hypertension, not routine oral control.
500.D: Methyldopa is a first-line, safety-proven oral antihypertensive in pregnancy.
500.hy: Safe oral antihypertensives in pregnancy: methyldopa, labetalol, nifedipine; avoid ACE inhibitors and ARBs.
500.one: Chronic hypertension in pregnancy = methyldopa (first-line oral).

501.A: Prolonged rupture of membranes with fever, uterine tenderness, and foul purulent discharge is intra-amniotic infection (chorioamnionitis).
501.B: A UTI causes dysuria and flank pain, not foul vaginal discharge with rupture of membranes.
501.C: Antepartum hemorrhage causes bleeding, not infection.
501.D: A lower respiratory tract infection involves unrelated respiratory symptoms.
501.hy: Prolonged rupture of membranes + fever + uterine tenderness + foul discharge = chorioamnionitis: give antibiotics and deliver.
501.one: Prolonged ROM + fever + foul discharge = Chorioamnionitis.

502.A: Parental karyotyping detects chromosomal causes (e.g., a balanced translocation) of recurrent first-trimester loss.
502.B: Hysteroscopy assesses the uterine cavity, more relevant to second-trimester/anatomic loss.
502.C: Pelvic ultrasound is limited for the etiology of recurrent early loss.
502.D: Hysterosalpingography evaluates tubal and uterine anatomy, more relevant to later losses.
502.hy: Recurrent first-trimester miscarriage workup includes parental karyotyping (structural chromosomal abnormalities) and antiphospholipid antibody testing.

503.A: Androgens do affect the developing genitalia, so there is an effect.
503.B: Hirsutism is a maternal/adult effect, not the fetal genital effect.
503.D: Androgenic progestins virilize (masculinize) the external genitalia of a female fetus.
503.hy: Androgenic progestins can masculinize (virilize) the external genitalia of a female fetus.
503.one: Androgenic progestin in pregnancy = masculinization of female fetal genitalia.

505.A: Observation fails to prevent vertical transmission.
505.B: Vaccination alone is insufficient without immunoglobulin.
505.C: Immunoglobulin alone is insufficient without active vaccination.
505.D: Infants of HBsAg-positive mothers receive both active (vaccine) and passive (HBIG) immunoprophylaxis within 12 hours of birth.
505.hy: An infant of an HBsAg-positive mother should get the hepatitis B vaccine plus HBIG within 12 hours of birth.
505.one: HBsAg-positive mother = infant gets HepB vaccine + immunoglobulin.

506.A: Tocolysis with atosiban is futile at 6 cm dilation (established labor).
506.B: Antenatal corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity are the most beneficial intervention when preterm delivery is imminent.
506.C: Antibiotics are given only if infection or GBS is indicated and are not the key drug here.
506.D: Magnesium neuroprotection is useful, but corticosteroids are the priority answer.
506.hy: Imminent preterm birth: antenatal corticosteroids (before 34 weeks) are the single most beneficial intervention for fetal lung maturity; tocolysis is futile in advanced labor.
506.one: Preterm labor at 27 weeks, 6 cm dilated = give corticosteroids (steroids).

507.A: Phenytoin is inferior to magnesium sulfate for eclampsia.
507.B: Nifedipine controls blood pressure, not seizures.
507.C: Hydralazine controls severe hypertension, not the seizure.
507.D: Magnesium sulfate is the drug of choice to control and prevent eclamptic seizures.
507.hy: Magnesium sulfate is first-line to treat and prevent eclamptic seizures (and for neuroprotection).

508.A: Symptomatic placenta previa with recurrent bleeding at 37 weeks requires immediate admission for monitoring and delivery planning.
508.B: No admission is unsafe with active bleeding and previa.
508.C: Admission should not be delayed until after planning the cesarean.
508.D: Bleeding has already recurred, so admit now rather than waiting for another episode.
508.hy: Symptomatic placenta previa with bleeding: admit immediately; deliver by cesarean with timing based on bleeding and gestation.
508.one: Bleeding placenta previa at term = admit immediately.

509.A: Conservative management of prolonged term rupture of membranes without labor increases infection risk.
509.C: There is no indication for cesarean (cephalic, reassuring status).
509.D: Term rupture of membranes without spontaneous labor should be managed by induction to reduce chorioamnionitis risk.
509.hy: Term PROM (37 weeks or more) without labor: induce labor to reduce infection risk.
509.one: Term ROM, no labor after 24 h = induce labor.

510.A: Placenta previa is a low-lying placenta, not the abnormal adherence described.
510.B: Placenta increta invades into the myometrium.
510.C: Abnormal placental adherence to the uterine wall, strongly associated with prior cesarean sections, is placenta accreta.
510.D: Placenta percreta penetrates through the serosa into adjacent organs.
510.hy: Placenta accreta spectrum (accreta/increta/percreta) is abnormal placental adherence/invasion; risk rises with prior cesareans and placenta previa.
510.one: Abnormally adherent placenta + prior cesareans = Placenta accreta.

511.A: Observation is inadequate because the definitive treatment of eclampsia is delivery.
511.C: Eclampsia mandates delivery once the mother is stabilized, regardless of gestation.
511.D: After stabilizing (seizure and BP control), deliver, as delivery is the only cure for eclampsia.
511.hy: Eclampsia: stabilize (magnesium sulfate, BP control) then deliver; delivery is the definitive treatment regardless of gestational age.

512.A: Atosiban is an oxytocin-antagonist tocolytic and is not the priority once steroids are underway and neuroprotection is indicated.
512.B: Ritodrine is a beta-agonist tocolytic with significant maternal side effects and is not the best choice.
512.C: Nifedipine is a tocolytic, but before 32 weeks magnesium for neuroprotection is the key drug.
512.D: Magnesium sulfate provides fetal neuroprotection (reduces cerebral palsy) in preterm labor before 32 weeks.
512.hy: Magnesium sulfate is given for fetal neuroprotection in threatened preterm birth before 32 weeks.
512.one: Preterm labor before 32 weeks = magnesium sulfate (neuroprotection).

513.A: After an antepartum bleed, assess fetal wellbeing first with CTG (cardiotocography).
513.B: Ultrasound confirms the placental site, but fetal monitoring comes first.
513.C: A biophysical profile is more detailed, but CTG is the immediate assessment.
513.D: Umbilical artery Doppler is for growth-restriction surveillance, not acute bleed assessment.
513.hy: After antepartum hemorrhage, immediate assessment of fetal wellbeing is with CTG.

514.A: Oxytocin augments contractions but does not ripen an unfavorable cervix.
514.B: Methergine is a uterotonic for PPH, not for ripening, and is contraindicated before delivery.
514.C: Syntometrine is a uterotonic for third-stage management, not for ripening.
514.D: Prostaglandin E2 (dinoprostone) ripens an unfavorable (closed, long) cervix before induction.
514.hy: An unfavorable cervix (low Bishop score) is ripened with prostaglandin E2 (dinoprostone) before oxytocin induction.
514.one: Unfavorable cervix for induction = Prostaglandin E2 (ripening).

515.A: Smoking is the most significant and modifiable risk factor here, associated with IUGR, abruption, and stillbirth.
515.B: Maternal age of 35 is a mild risk, less significant than active smoking.
515.C: A second-degree family history of diabetes is a weak risk.
515.D: Previous subfertility is a minor obstetric risk.
515.hy: Smoking in pregnancy is a major modifiable risk factor (IUGR, placental abruption, preterm birth, stillbirth).
515.one: Pregnancy risk factors: smoking is the most significant (and modifiable).

516.A: Observation is inappropriate with abruption and intrauterine fetal death; deliver.
516.B: With a dead fetus and a stable mother, vaginal delivery is preferred; cesarean adds surgical risk.
516.C: The cervix is only 5 cm and not fully dilated, so instrumental delivery is not possible.
516.D: With fetal death and a partly dilated cervix, augment labor for vaginal delivery while monitoring for coagulopathy.
516.hy: Abruption with fetal demise and a stable mother: aim for vaginal delivery (augment labor); reserve cesarean for maternal instability or obstruction.
516.one: Abruption + IUFD + stable mother = augment labor for vaginal delivery.

517.A: Atenolol is avoided in pregnancy because of association with IUGR.
517.B: Labetalol is a first-line antihypertensive for severe hypertension in pregnancy/preeclampsia.
517.C: Diazoxide is rarely used and causes hyperglycemia and marked hypotension.
517.D: Sodium nitroprusside risks fetal cyanide toxicity and is avoided.
517.hy: Labetalol (or hydralazine/nifedipine) is used for severe hypertension in pregnancy; avoid atenolol, nitroprusside, ACE inhibitors, and ARBs.

518.A: Oliguria is not a typical effect of beta-mimetics.
518.B: Beta-mimetics (ritodrine, terbutaline) cause tachycardia and palpitations via beta-1 stimulation.
518.C: Abdominal pain is not a characteristic effect.
518.D: Vaginal bleeding is unrelated to beta-mimetic use.
518.hy: Beta-mimetic tocolytics cause maternal tachycardia/palpitations, tremor, hyperglycemia, and pulmonary edema.

519.A: Placenta previa causes painless, not painful, bleeding.
519.B: Cervical cancer does not fit this acute picture.
519.C: Painful vaginal bleeding with contractions and fetal distress (late decelerations), with smoking as a risk factor, is placental abruption.
519.D: A congenital bleeding disorder does not fit localized painful bleeding.
519.hy: Placental abruption = painful antepartum bleeding with uterine tenderness/contractions and possible fetal distress; risks include smoking, hypertension, and trauma.
519.one: Painful antepartum bleeding + contractions + distress = Abruptio placentae.

520.A: Nifedipine is a tocolytic, but at this gestation magnesium for neuroprotection is the keyed choice.
520.B: Terbutaline is a beta-agonist tocolytic with more side effects.
520.C: Indomethacin is a tocolytic limited to before 32 weeks but risks premature ductal closure and oligohydramnios.
520.D: Magnesium sulfate provides fetal neuroprotection in preterm labor before 32 weeks.
520.hy: Magnesium sulfate for fetal neuroprotection is indicated in threatened preterm labor before 32 weeks.
520.one: Preterm labor at 31 weeks = magnesium sulfate (neuroprotection).

521.A: Painless antepartum bleeding with a soft, non-tender uterus (with prior cesareans raising the risk) is placenta previa.
521.B: Uterine rupture causes severe pain, fetal distress, and hemodynamic collapse.
521.C: A cervical laceration is usually associated with trauma or labor.
521.D: Placental abruption causes painful bleeding with a tense, tender uterus.
521.hy: Placenta previa = painless antepartum bleeding with a soft, non-tender uterus; risk rises with prior cesareans. Diagnose by ultrasound and avoid digital exam.
521.one: Painless antepartum bleeding + soft uterus = Placenta previa.

522.A: New hypertensive features (headache, visual symptoms) with proteinuria after 20 weeks is preeclampsia.
522.B: Proteinuria alone does not explain the acute neuro-visual symptoms in pregnancy.
522.C: Pyelonephritis causes fever, flank pain, and dysuria, not this picture.
522.D: Chronic hypertension predates 20 weeks and lacks new proteinuria.
522.hy: Preeclampsia = new-onset hypertension plus proteinuria (or end-organ dysfunction) after 20 weeks; severe features include headache and visual disturbance.
522.one: New hypertension + proteinuria + visual symptoms after 20 weeks = Preeclampsia.

523.A: Vasa previa causes painless bleeding with fetal (not maternal) blood at membrane rupture.
523.B: Placenta previa causes painless bleeding with a non-tender uterus.
523.C: Uterine rupture is usually intrapartum with a uterine scar, severe pain, and collapse.
523.D: Painful vaginal bleeding with uterine tenderness is placental abruption.
523.hy: Painful antepartum hemorrhage with a tender uterus is placental abruption; painless bleeding is placenta previa.
523.one: Painful bleeding + uterine tenderness = Abruptio placentae.

524.A: Atosiban is an oxytocin antagonist with no effect on the ductus.
524.B: Ritodrine is a beta-agonist with no ductal effect.
524.C: Nifedipine is a calcium channel blocker with no ductal effect.
524.D: Indomethacin is an NSAID/prostaglandin inhibitor that acts on the fetal ductus arteriosus (premature constriction/closure), which is why its use is limited to short courses before 32 weeks.
524.hy: Indomethacin (a prostaglandin synthase inhibitor) affects the fetal ductus arteriosus (premature constriction) and can cause oligohydramnios; use it short-term and before 32 weeks.
524.one: Tocolytic acting on the fetal ductus arteriosus = Indomethacin.

525.A: Vacuum is contraindicated in prematurity because of the fragile fetal head.
525.B: A vertex-presenting preterm fetus with a reassuring CTG can deliver vaginally.
525.C: There is no obstetric indication for cesarean.
525.D: Forceps are relatively contraindicated in prematurity.
525.hy: Preterm vertex presentation with reassuring status delivers vaginally; avoid vacuum and forceps because of the fragile preterm head.
525.one: Preterm vertex + reassuring CTG = vaginal delivery.

526.A: There is no time for steroids with massive hemorrhage and fetal distress.
526.B: Tocolytics are contraindicated with hemorrhage and distress.
526.C: Vaginal delivery is too slow for fetal distress with maternal hemorrhage.
526.D: Massive antepartum hemorrhage with fetal distress requires immediate delivery by cesarean.
526.hy: Massive antepartum hemorrhage with fetal distress or maternal instability requires an emergency cesarean delivery.
526.one: Massive APH + fetal distress = emergency cesarean.

527.A: Oxygen is not needed in a stable patient with a normal CTG.
527.B: Stable previa with bleeding at 27 weeks warrants antenatal corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity while managing expectantly.
527.C: Tocolytics are not indicated without established labor and should be used with caution when bleeding.
527.D: There is no infection indication for antibiotics.
527.hy: Stable preterm placenta previa with bleeding: admit, give antenatal corticosteroids (before 34 weeks), manage expectantly, and deliver by cesarean.
527.one: Stable previa bleeding at 27 weeks = antenatal corticosteroids.

528.A: A first-degree tear involves skin/vaginal mucosa only.
528.B: A second-degree tear involves the perineal muscles, not the anal sphincter.
528.C: A third-degree tear involves the anal sphincter complex but not the rectal mucosa.
528.D: A fourth-degree tear extends through the anal sphincter into the rectal mucosa.
528.hy: Perineal tears: 1st = skin, 2nd = perineal muscle, 3rd = anal sphincter, 4th = rectal mucosa.
528.one: Laceration through rectal mucosa = Fourth-degree tear.

529.A: Renal agenesis causes oligohydramnios because of absent fetal urine.
529.B: Rh isoimmunization can cause hydrops/polyhydramnios but is less common than diabetes.
529.C: Fetal hyperglycemia from uncontrolled maternal diabetes causes osmotic diuresis and polyhydramnios, a common cause.
529.hy: Common causes of polyhydramnios: maternal diabetes, fetal anomalies (GI atresia, anencephaly), and multiple gestation; renal agenesis and IUGR cause oligohydramnios.
529.one: Polyhydramnios: common cause is uncontrolled maternal diabetes.

530.A: Folic acid is useful, but at 14 weeks the neural-tube period has passed and the priority is medication safety.
530.B: An ECG is not the immediate priority.
530.C: Dietitian advice is a helpful adjunct, not the key step.
530.D: Review her chronic hypertension and diabetes medications for teratogens (stop ACE inhibitors/ARBs/statins; switch to pregnancy-safe agents).
530.hy: At booking, review medications in women with chronic disease: stop teratogens (ACE inhibitors/ARBs, statins, certain oral hypoglycemics) and switch to pregnancy-safe drugs.
530.one: Pregnant with chronic HTN/diabetes = review/adjust medications (stop teratogens).

531.A: A complex adnexal mass with acute pain is further characterized with pelvic MRI to assess for malignancy or torsion.
531.B: Observation is unsafe with acute severe pain and a complex mass.
531.C: Laparotomy is premature before characterizing the mass.
531.D: Tumor markers are an adjunct, but imaging characterization is the next step.
531.hy: A complex adnexal mass needs characterization (ultrasound then MRI) and tumor markers (CA-125) to assess malignancy risk before surgery.
531.one: Complex adnexal mass + acute pain = characterize with pelvic MRI.

532.A: In a young woman, an ovarian cyst/complex mass is managed with ovary-sparing cystectomy (to preserve fertility) plus histology.
532.B: Oophorectomy unnecessarily sacrifices the ovary in a young woman.
532.C: A 6 cm complex, solid mass warrants surgical removal, not observation.
532.D: Ultrasound-guided aspiration risks spillage and is not appropriate for a complex, solid mass.
532.hy: In young women, persistent or complex ovarian masses are managed with ovary-sparing cystectomy to preserve fertility, with histology.
532.one: Young woman + complex ovarian mass = cystectomy (ovary-sparing).

533.A: Cystectomy is inadequate for a likely malignant mass in an elderly woman.
533.B: Oophorectomy alone is insufficient staging if malignancy is present.
533.C: A complex, solid adnexal mass in a postmenopausal woman is high-risk for malignancy and should be referred to gynecologic oncology for staging surgery.
533.D: Definitive surgery should be done by or with gynecologic oncology after referral and staging planning.
533.hy: A complex adnexal mass in a postmenopausal woman is presumed malignant until proven otherwise: refer to gynecologic oncology for staging.
533.one: Postmenopausal complex adnexal mass = refer to gynecologic oncology.
